div.p: How jumps create basic blocks and CFG edges

The Revelation: The VM executes linear bytecode. The CFG exists only in the compiler's analysis. When you see while (n > 0), the compiler creates: condition block → body block → back-edge. The VM just sees jumps. Structure is imposed by the compiler, not the machine.

g.text: JUMP target | creates edge to target block
fall-through | implicit edge to next block

div.p: Jumps define basic block boundaries. Each JUMP target starts a new block. Each JUMP instruction ends its block.

g.text: 3. Instruction after JUMP is a leader

text.tspan: 3. Instruction after JUMP is a leader

g.text: Source Code → AST → Bytecode → CFG → Optimizations → Native Code

text.tspan: Source Code → AST → Bytecode → CFG → Optimizations → Native Code

div.p: Data Flow Analysis: Reaching definitions, live variables Optimizations: Dead code elimination, constant propagation Loop Detection: Identify loops via back-edges for unrolling Register Allocation: Compute liveness across blocks
Notice how JUMP targets (6, 32) define block boundaries. The backward JUMP at 26 creates the loop back-edge BB2 → BB1.
